The most famous idea of this critic was attacked in (*) Pauline Kael’s essay “Circles and Squares,” and this critic is often framed as Kael’s main rival. This figure was created shortly after his creator moved to Los Angeles, where he binged on cocaine, milk, and hot peppers. “Cold & Well-Lit Place” and “The Finished Line” appear on a 2017 album by experimental band Oxbow whose title is an adjective-flipped reference to this figure. In a notorious interview, this figure said that (*) Hitler was “quite as good as Jagger” and was “the first rock star.” This figure appears “throwing darts in lovers’ eyes” during his “return” mentioned several times in the song “Station to Station.” Perceptions of this persona were colored by a photograph in which he appeared to give a Nazi salute while touring for the album Young Americans. A number sequence on a suit in this work is likely a reference to Psalm 137, Verse 9. A passage in this work compares the Greek conception of the Fates to Leonardo da Vinci’s Madonna of the Yarnwinder. In this work, the story of Noah’s Ark is contrasted with the Chinese myth of siblings surviving the flood by hiding in a gourd by a prepper referred to as The Novelist’s Son. A character in this work who shows off a copy of Charlie Chaplin’s directorial debut Twenty Minutes of Love has his (*) heart stopped every 21 minutes. A tool in this work is named after a wheel-like creature from Franz Kafka’s story “The Cares of a Family Man” and is called the Odradek. This work’s epigraph is a Kobo Abe quote mentioning the usefulness of the stick and the rope. Though it was developed before it occurred, many commentators have commended this work for evoking the COVID pandemic, though others have mocked on-the-nose character names like Die-Hardman. This game’s two-part novelization attempts to explain concepts like timefall, DOOMs, the chiral network, and Bridge babies. 676a112497acc2cc958b2fea.question: This object, which is on permanent display in a state university system’s Museum of the North, was airlifted out of its original position to prevent tourists from trying to reach it. The final image in a 1996 nonfiction book is of two grieving parents seeing this object grow smaller as they leave an area by helicopter; that book, whose cover art usually features this object half buried in snow, also discusses the disappearance of Everett Ruess in Utah. One theory holds that a man who (*) died inside this object succumbed to toxins found in the Eskimo potatoes he found growing nearby. That man, nicknamed Supertramp, pinned a page of Gogol to this object before being blocked by a raging river caused by snowmelt and after discovering this temporary lodging when hitchhiking up the Stampede Trail. For 10 points, name this immobile vehicle located in the Alaskan wilderness where Chris McCandless died, as documented in Jon Krakauer’s book Into the Wild.

For 10 points, name this immobile vehicle located in the Alaskan wilderness where Chris McCandless died, as documented in Jon Krakauer's book Into the Wild.

One of these people learned that the other was still alive and lived nearby from Vladimir Orlov while in the back room of a bookstore. These two people met and talked about life and literature all night in an oft-mythologized 1945 meeting, after which one of them collapsed and said “I am in love.” One of these people wrote about the other, who at the time was the UK ambassador to Russia, as “The Guest from the Future” in her (*) Poem Without A Hero. 676a112497acc2cc958b300d.question: This thinker wrote that since noise makes thinking impossible, humans should have been born with air-tight flaps over their ears like those on bats if they were intended to be thinking creatures. This thinker imagined a dog saying “you are not my master, but my Devil” in a passage in which he gleefully recalls an abused dog mauling a lord. This thinker wrote that a “quick test of the assertion that enjoyment outweighs pain in this world [...] would be to compare the feelings of an animal engaged in eating another with those of the animal being eaten.” This thinker wrote “compassion for animals is intimately associated with goodness of character, and it may be confidently asserted that he who is cruel to animals cannot be a good man” in his book The (*) Basis of Morality. This thinker wrote that one of his central ideas is exemplified by an animal that will fight with itself after being sliced in half; that animal is the Australian bull-ant. This philosopher owned a succession of poodles all named Atman, reflecting his interest in Indian thought. For 10 points, name this philosopher who often mentioned the suffering of animals in The World as Will and Idea.

676a112497acc2cc958b3021.question: The amnesiac protagonist, who forgets his life but remembers arcane literary facts, wakes up in Guanajuato with an ID card indicating that he is one of these figures in Jim Gauer’s Novel Explosives. Lesser known examples of these figures include the Baron of Tieve, whose insistence on living only according to reason led him to commit suicide, and the long-bearded astrologer Raphael Baldaya. One of these figures wrote “How should I know what I'll be, I who don't know what I am?” in the poem “The (*) Tobacco Shop.”One of these figures studied naval engineering in Scotland before becoming a Futurist and then a sentimentalist who wrote about his childhood. An Epicurean one of these figures exiled to Brazil following a failed 1919 rebellion talks to the ghost of the author who created him in a 1984 novel. Jose Saramago wrote that novel, titled for the “Year of the Death of” one of these figures. For 10 points, Alberto Caeiro, Bernardo Soares, and Ricardo Reis are among what set of names denoting differing styles used by the Portuguese author of The Book of Disquiet?
